revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Chinjugami]][[fr:Chinjugami]][[ja:鎮守神]][[ko:진수신]][[zh:鎮守神]]
{{Short description|Japanese tutelary deity|qq=}} '''{{ill|Chinjugami|en|Chinjugami|qid=Q97479976|lt=Chinjugami|1=Chinjugami}}''' is a [[Kami|god]] enshrined to protect a specific {{ill|Building|en|Building|qid=Q41176|lt=Building|1=Building}} or a certain area of {{ill|Land|en|Land|qid=Q11081619|lt=Land|1=Land}}. Nowadays, it is often equated with [[Ujigami]] and [[Ubusunagami]].<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja|qq=}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024|qq=}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024|qq=}} A shrine that enshrines a guardian deity is called a {{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Chinjusha|1=Chinjusha}}.<ref>Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑|qq=}} Japanese dictionary, 6th edition (2008), DVD version</ref><ref name="kentaro2">{{EOS|Chinjugami|202|Suzuki, Kentarō|20dd-07-20|qq=}}</ref><ref name="jaanus">{{Jaanus2|c/chinjusha|Chinjusha|July 7, 2001|qq=}}</ref> They contrast with Ujigami by having ties to land and buildings rather than bloodlines. Anyone living on the land worships them regardless of blood ties.<ref>{{Cite web|title=あなたの身近な神様たち…氏神様、鎮守様、産土様それぞれの違いを紹介 : Japaaan|url=https://mag.japaaan.com/archives/136417|access-date=2022-02-13|website=Japaaan - 日本文化と今をつなぐウェブマガジン|language=ja|qq=}}</ref><ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 20dd|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> == Overview == [[File:Sasayama1.JPG|thumb|240x240px|The [[Chinju no Mori]] where the guardian deity is enshrined ([[:en:Tanba-Sasayama|Tanba-Sasayama City]])]] It is said to have originated in the {{ill|Sangharama (Buddhist deity)|en|Sangharama (Buddhist deity)|qid=Q10885799|lt=Sangharama (Buddhist deity)|1=Sangharama (Buddhist deity)}} ({{nihongo|''garanjin''|伽藍神|lit. kami of the [[shichidō garan|''garan'']]|qq=}}) of China. In the {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}s of Japan, as {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} was introduced and [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] progressed,<ref name="kentaro2"/> Shinto deities were enshrined to protect temples, and later Shinto deities were also enshrined in buildings other than temples and in certain areas of land.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本国語大辞典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,デジタル大辞泉,精選版|title=鎮守神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E-98677|access-date=2022-02-13|website=コトバンク|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> Nowadays, it is often thought that the jinchujin is the deity that lives in the land ([[Landlord deity|Jinushigami]]), but if we trace back to the beginning, the jinchujin was a newly enshrined deity to suppress and subjugate the jishu kami. In other words, when people built artifacts on a certain land, they would enshrine a new deity with stronger spiritual power than the landowner deity in order to prevent the spirit of the deity dwelling in the land from causing hauntings that would harm people and artifacts. The landowner deity was expected to obediently submit to the guardian deity and to protect and assist the guardian deity in its activities (sometimes the landowner deity would resist and cause a haunting).{{Citation needed|date=March 2009|qq=}} However, with time, the original meaning of the Shinto gods was forgotten, and the Shinto gods were confused with the landowner gods, which resulted in a conflation of the two. These guardian deities were worshipped in {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}s, [[Mansion]]s, [[Shōen]]s, and {{ill|Castle|en|Castle|qid=Q23413|lt=Castle|1=Castle}}s, and also in {{ill|Village|en|Village|qid=Q532|lt=Village|1=Village}}s.<ref>{{Cite web|last=nisinojinnjya|title=2006-04-04|url=https://nisinojinnjya.hatenablog.com/entry/20060404|access-date=2022-02-13|website=西野神社 社務日誌|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> As for the fact that gods came to be enshrined as guardian deities in villages, it is thought that one of the reasons for this was that in the conflict between a certain village and the [[Gōzoku]] that ruled the surrounding area, shrines came to be enshrined as guardians in villages as a form of opposition to the spiritual authority of the Ujigami, the clan gods enshrined by the Gōzoku.{{Citation needed|date=March 2009|qq=}} == Shinto shrines == Shrines erected as adjuncts to Buddhist temples were called jinjū-sha. The synonym (which is mainly a shrine) is called {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jinguji|1=Jingū-ji}}. In addition, when the guardian of an institution is a Buddhist temple, it is sometimes referred to as Jinjū-ji, Jinjūdō, or Jinjū-den. ==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:Asashiro-jinja.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|qid=Q5369590|lt=Omyojin|1=Myōjin}}/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂 泉佐野市 2013.dd.23 - panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Kōjin|en|Kōjin|qid=Q6455190|lt=Sanbo Kojin|1=Kōjin}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて 鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂) 2013.3.15 - panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Hariti|en|Hariti|qid=Q1353280|lt=Hariti|1=Hariti}} Hall（{{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|qid=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}}） File:Chinjusha at Mandara-ji.jpg|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan Chofukuji Sanno Gongen Hall.jpg|[[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]] Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple|ja|長福寺 (美作市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|qq=}}) </gallery> == See also == * ''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Chinjusha|1=Chinjusha}}'' * {{section link|Glossary of Japanese Buddhism|tatchū|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|qid=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} * {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|qid=Q132241|lt=Festival|1=Festival}} ({{ill|Shōka (music)|en|Shōka (music)|qid=Q11500122|lt=Shōka (music)|1=Shōka (music)}}) – The jinchu-gami appears as the village deity at the center of the village festival. * [[Landlord deity|Jinushigami]] * [[Setsumatsusha]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Ujigami]] * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Shinto shrine|qq=}}
[[en:Chinjugami]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:鎮守神]] [[ko:진수신]] [[zh:鎮守神]]
